meta.title: OAI-6: Evaluation, Grounding, and Safe Automation

questions.stem: A pilot project exposed a design mistake. A team wants an assistant to break a large task into smaller steps, use tools when needed, and return a final answer. What application pattern does this describe MOST closely?

options.text: A static image-only pipeline
A pure keyword search index with no model reasoning
An agent-style workflow
A CSS theming system

questions.explanation: **An agent-style workflow** is correct because agentic applications plan, use tools, and iterate toward a result. The other options do not describe a model-driven, tool-using workflow.

questions.stem:
A communications specialist wants ChatGPT to produce a 150-word executive summary. The model consistently returns 400-word summaries.

What is the MOST direct fix?

options.text: Manually trim the response to 150 words
Use a different AI tool for length-controlled writing
Ask the model to count words as it writes
Add "Respond in exactly 150 words" to the prompt and verify the length after generation

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because an explicit word count instruction in the prompt is the most direct control mechanism. Models do not always hit exact word counts precisely, so verifying length after generation and iterating is a standard workflow.

**A** is incorrect because instructing the model to count as it writes is not a reliable mechanism; models generate text, not count in real time.

**C** is incorrect because manual trimming works but is time-consuming and bypasses the prompt improvement that would make future requests more efficient.

**D** is incorrect because length control is a common feature of all major language models and is addressed through prompt design.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A customer success manager receives a ChatGPT response that is mostly correct but too long and includes unnecessary background information.

What is the BEST follow-up action?

options.text: Start a new conversation and try a completely different prompt
Follow up in the same conversation asking the model to shorten the response and remove the background section
Accept the response as-is since AI cannot be refined
Switch to a different AI vendor

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because iterative refinement in the same conversation is the standard workflow for improving AI outputs. The model retains context and can trim or restructure its previous response when given specific follow-up instructions.

**A** is incorrect because starting over discards the conversational context and the partially useful output; follow-up prompting is more efficient.

**C** is incorrect because AI outputs are meant to be refined through iteration; accepting a suboptimal response is not a best practice.

**D** is incorrect because length and relevance issues are addressed through prompt refinement, not by changing vendors.

[OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)

questions.stem:
A policy writer needs to draft a company AI acceptable use policy. She asks ChatGPT to write the policy from scratch with no additional inputs.

What is the MOST significant limitation of this approach?

options.text: The policy will be too long for practical use
The model will generate the policy in legal language that employees cannot understand
ChatGPT cannot write policy documents

The model will generate a generic policy template that does not reflect the company's specific AI tools, risk profile, industry regulations, or cultural values — significant customization will be required

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a policy generated without company-specific context — which AI tools are deployed, what data is handled, the industry's regulatory environment, and the organization's risk tolerance — will be a generic template requiring substantial rework before adoption.

**A** is incorrect because policy drafting is a common and productive use case for ChatGPT.

**C** is incorrect because length is a stylistic issue that can be addressed with a prompt instruction; it is not the primary limitation.

**D** is incorrect because the model can produce plain-language policies; the issue is content specificity, not language register.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A marketing manager asks ChatGPT to "write something about our new product" and receives a vague two-sentence response that doesn't match the company's needs.

What is the MOST likely cause of this poor output?

options.text: Marketing prompts require a paid ChatGPT subscription
ChatGPT is not capable of writing marketing content
The prompt lacks specificity about audience, tone, format, and product details
The model's temperature setting is too low

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because vague prompts produce vague outputs. Without specifying the target audience, desired tone, output format (e.g., social post vs. email), and product details, the model has no basis for producing useful content.

**A** is incorrect because ChatGPT is well-suited for marketing copywriting when given sufficient direction.

**C** is incorrect because temperature affects creativity and randomness but does not cause a short or off-target response from an under-specified prompt.

**D** is incorrect because prompt specificity is a technique issue unrelated to subscription tier.

[OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)

questions.stem: An implementation review is underway. A company is deploying a customer-facing AI assistant and wants to reduce misuse and harmful outputs. Which practice BEST aligns with official safety guidance?

options.text: Assume the model alone provides complete safety coverage
Add testing, monitoring, and guardrails around the application
Rely only on the first prompt written during prototyping
Disable all logging and evaluation so failures stay hidden

questions.explanation: **Add testing, monitoring, and guardrails around the application** is correct because safety best practices emphasize layered controls, evaluation, and monitoring. A single prompt or blind trust in the model is not enough for a production safety strategy.

questions.stem:
A team is reviewing its approach. A legal operations team needs the model to return extracted contract obligations in a fixed JSON shape every time.

Which technique BEST supports that requirement?

options.text: Ask the model to be creative so it explores more formats
Remove examples so the model has fewer constraints
Use Structured Outputs with a schema that defines the expected JSON fields
Split the answer across multiple unrelated conversations

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because Structured Outputs are designed to make model responses conform to a defined schema.

**A** is incorrect because creativity increases variability instead of enforcing a fixed shape.

**B** is incorrect because removing constraints reduces consistency.

**D** is incorrect because separating context does not enforce schema compliance.

questions.stem: Before rolling out a new prompt change, a product team wants an evidence-based way to compare answer quality on representative tasks. What should they do?

options.text: Change the prompt in production without measurement
Judge quality only from one impressive demo
Use a brighter UI theme to improve model accuracy
Run evaluations on a representative test set

questions.explanation: **Run evaluations on a representative test set** is correct because evaluations provide a repeatable way to compare prompt or system changes. One-off demos and cosmetic changes do not measure model quality reliably.

questions.stem: A team wants an assistant to break a large task into smaller steps, use tools when needed, and return a final answer. What application pattern does this describe MOST closely?

options.text: An agent-style workflow
A pure keyword search index with no model reasoning
A CSS theming system
A static image-only pipeline

questions.explanation: **An agent-style workflow** is correct because agentic applications plan, use tools, and iterate toward a result. The other options do not describe a model-driven, tool-using workflow.

questions.stem:
A recruiter uses ChatGPT to draft outreach messages for open engineering roles. She notices all messages sound identical despite instructions to personalize them.

What context would MOST help the model produce personalized messages?

options.text: The recruiter's name and company logo
Each candidate's relevant skills, recent projects or articles, and the specific role detail that matches their background
The number of open roles at the company
The recruiter's LinkedIn profile URL

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because genuine personalization requires candidate-specific details — their skills, something they've built or written, and a clear link to the role. Without these inputs, the model has nothing to personalize with and defaults to generic phrasing.

**A** is incorrect because the recruiter's name and logo are sender identity elements, not personalization content for the recipient.

**C** is incorrect because the headcount of open roles is internal information irrelevant to the candidate's personalized message.

**D** is incorrect because a LinkedIn URL is a link to the recruiter, not candidate-specific context for personalization.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. A project manager likes the first draft overall, but it is too long and buries the action items.

Which follow-up is MOST effective?

options.text: Accept the first output because iteration rarely helps
Delete the earlier context so the model cannot refer to the draft
Start over with a different vendor before refining the draft
Continue in the same conversation and ask for a shorter version that highlights action items

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because iterative refinement is a standard way to improve length, focus, and formatting.

**A** is incorrect because AI drafts are meant to be iterated on.

**B** is incorrect because discarding useful context can make revision harder.

**C** is incorrect because switching vendors does not address a prompt-refinement issue.

questions.stem:
A research analyst uses ChatGPT to summarize a 50-page industry report and asks: "Summarize this report." The model produces a summary that seems accurate but omits several findings the analyst considers important.

What should the analyst do to get a more complete summary?

options.text: Ask the model to rewrite the summary from scratch
Specify which sections or topics are most important and ask the model to ensure those are covered
Submit the report in multiple separate messages
Accept the summary as complete since AI selects the most important points

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because AI models make their own judgments about salience when given open-ended summarization instructions. By explicitly naming the sections or topics that must appear, the analyst overrides the model's defaults and gets a summary aligned with her priorities.

**A** is incorrect because rewriting from scratch without new instructions will likely produce a similar selection of topics.

**C** is incorrect because splitting the report into separate messages loses cross-section context and complicates the task.

**D** is incorrect because AI salience judgments may not match domain expert priorities; specifying requirements is essential for professional use.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A software engineer wants ChatGPT to consistently format all its code review feedback as a numbered list with a severity label (Critical / Major / Minor) before each item.

Where should this formatting requirement be placed for the MOST reliable results?

options.text: At the end of every user message as a reminder
In the system prompt so it applies to every response
In a separate follow-up message after receiving the initial response
In the code comments submitted for review

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the system prompt establishes persistent instructions that govern all responses in the session. Formatting rules placed there are applied consistently without needing to repeat them.

**A** is incorrect because embedding the requirement in every user message is repetitive and easy to forget; it also adds noise to the actual content of the request.

**C** is incorrect because asking for a reformat after receiving a response creates extra steps and is not scalable across many reviews.

**D** is incorrect because code comments are part of the content being reviewed, not instructions to the model.

[OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)

questions.stem: A pilot project exposed a design mistake. An application must return JSON that always conforms to a required schema so downstream code can parse it safely. Which feature BEST supports that?

options.text: Structured Outputs with a supplied schema
A higher temperature setting
A longer free-form prompt with no schema
Multiple unrelated conversations for the same task

questions.explanation: **Structured Outputs with a supplied schema** is correct because it is designed to make model responses conform to a JSON Schema. A free-form prompt, higher temperature, or splitting context across conversations does not guarantee schema adherence.

questions.stem:
A support lead wants consistently useful answers from ChatGPT for refund-policy questions. Early outputs are vague because the requests omit the customer type, policy constraints, and expected response format.

Which change MOST directly improves output quality?

options.text: Add specific context, constraints, and the desired format to the prompt
Increase randomness so the model improvises more
Replace all prompts with one-word requests
Rely on a later follow-up to clarify every missing detail

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because clear instructions and relevant context are the foundation of effective prompting.

**B** is incorrect because higher randomness does not compensate for missing instructions.

**C** is incorrect because under-specified prompts usually worsen relevance.

**D** is incorrect because repeated cleanup is less reliable than providing the needed context upfront.

questions.stem:
A human resources professional is using ChatGPT to draft interview questions for a senior data analyst role. The first draft contains generic questions suitable for any role.

Which prompt revision would MOST improve relevance?

options.text: Ask for exactly 20 questions instead of the current number
Request that the model search the internet for interview templates
Add "make it better" to the original prompt
Specify the seniority level, key responsibilities, required technical skills, and the company's interview philosophy

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because providing role-specific context — seniority, key responsibilities, required skills, and interview style — gives the model the information it needs to produce targeted, relevant questions.

**A** is incorrect because "make it better" is as vague as the original prompt and gives the model no direction.

**C** is incorrect because changing the quantity does not improve relevance; you will get 20 generic questions instead of fewer generic ones.

**D** is incorrect because standard ChatGPT does not browse the internet (unless browsing is enabled), and the issue is prompt specificity, not content sourcing.

[OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)

questions.stem:
A communications specialist wants ChatGPT to produce a 150-word executive summary. The model consistently returns 400-word summaries.

What is the MOST direct fix?

options.text: Ask the model to count words as it writes
Add "Respond in exactly 150 words" to the prompt and verify the length after generation
Manually trim the response to 150 words
Use a different AI tool for length-controlled writing

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because an explicit word count instruction in the prompt is the most direct control mechanism. Models do not always hit exact word counts precisely, so verifying length after generation and iterating is a standard workflow.

**A** is incorrect because instructing the model to count as it writes is not a reliable mechanism; models generate text, not count in real time.

**C** is incorrect because manual trimming works but is time-consuming and bypasses the prompt improvement that would make future requests more efficient.

**D** is incorrect because length control is a common feature of all major language models and is addressed through prompt design.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem: A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. An application must return JSON that always conforms to a required schema so downstream code can parse it safely. Which feature BEST supports that?

options.text: Multiple unrelated conversations for the same task
Structured Outputs with a supplied schema
A higher temperature setting
A longer free-form prompt with no schema

questions.explanation: **Structured Outputs with a supplied schema** is correct because it is designed to make model responses conform to a JSON Schema. A free-form prompt, higher temperature, or splitting context across conversations does not guarantee schema adherence.

questions.stem:
A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. A chief of staff needs meeting notes transformed into a concise status update with decisions, risks, and next actions.

Which prompt change BEST improves the result?

options.text: Increase randomness so the notes become more diverse
Tell the model to respond however it prefers
Specify the required sections and audience for the summary
Ask for a summary with no instructions about structure or audience

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because summary quality improves when the desired structure and audience are explicit.

**A** is incorrect because higher randomness is not a substitute for clear requirements.

**B** is incorrect because removing guidance reduces consistency.

**D** is incorrect because missing structure leaves the model to guess.

questions.stem:
A business development executive asks ChatGPT to draft a competitive analysis comparing her company to three competitors. She provides no background about her company or the competitors.

What is the MOST likely problem with the output?

options.text: The model will refuse to write competitive analyses
The model will generate plausible-sounding but potentially inaccurate information about the competitors because it lacks grounding data
The output will be in a foreign language
The model cannot compare more than two companies at once

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because without grounding data, the model will draw on its training knowledge, which may be outdated, incomplete, or fabricated. Competitive intelligence requires current, sourced data — something the model cannot reliably provide from memory alone.

**A** is incorrect because competitive analysis is a common and accepted use case for ChatGPT.

**C** is incorrect because the model responds in the language of the prompt by default.

**D** is incorrect because the model can compare multiple entities; the issue is data quality, not quantity.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A technical writer asks ChatGPT to format its output as a table with columns: Feature, Description, and Availability. The model consistently returns bullet points instead.

Which approach is MOST likely to solve this?

options.text: Mention the format request at the very start of the prompt before any other content, and include an explicit example of the table header row
Ask the model to use a spreadsheet application instead
Submit the content in a CSV file for the model to convert
Use bold text in the prompt to emphasize the table request

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because placing the format requirement prominently at the beginning of the prompt — before the content — and showing an example header row (Feature | Description | Availability) gives the model unambiguous early signal about the expected structure.

**B** is incorrect because ChatGPT cannot open spreadsheet applications; the table formatting happens within the chat response.

**C** is incorrect because uploading a CSV is a different workflow and does not resolve an instruction-following issue with the current prompt.

**D** is incorrect because markdown bold in a prompt does not function as an emphasis signal to the model's instruction-following mechanism.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A legal operations team needs the model to return extracted contract obligations in a fixed JSON shape every time.

Which technique BEST supports that requirement?

options.text: Use Structured Outputs with a schema that defines the expected JSON fields
Ask the model to be creative so it explores more formats
Remove examples so the model has fewer constraints
Split the answer across multiple unrelated conversations

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Structured Outputs are designed to make model responses conform to a defined schema.

**B** is incorrect because creativity increases variability instead of enforcing a fixed shape.

**C** is incorrect because removing constraints reduces consistency.

**D** is incorrect because separating context does not enforce schema compliance.

questions.stem: An implementation review is underway. A builder reads only `output[0].content[0].text` and misses other generated output items. What is the safer approach described in the docs?

options.text: Convert every response to HTML before parsing it
Ignore tool calls because they never affect the response
Assume text is always stored in the first content item
Use the SDK convenience field such as `output_text` or handle the full output structure explicitly

questions.explanation: **Use the SDK convenience field such as `output_text` or handle the full output structure explicitly** is correct because the docs warn that output can contain multiple items, including tool calls and reasoning-related data. Assuming the first content item always contains the full text is unsafe.

questions.stem:
A sales team member wants to paste a prospect's full LinkedIn profile — including personal details about education, prior companies, and location — into ChatGPT to generate a tailored pitch.

What privacy consideration should she address first?

options.text: Whether sharing personally identifiable information about an individual with a third-party AI service is consistent with applicable privacy laws and company data policies
Whether the AI model has been trained on LinkedIn data
Whether the prospect prefers email over LinkedIn outreach
Whether the prospect's LinkedIn profile uses a professional photo

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because submitting another individual's personal information to a third-party AI service raises privacy considerations under regulations such as GDPR and CCPA, and may violate company data handling policies. The professional should verify compliance before proceeding.

**A** is incorrect because the prospect's photo is irrelevant to the privacy question.

**C** is incorrect because whether the model was trained on LinkedIn data does not determine whether submitting the profile is permissible.

**D** is incorrect because outreach channel preference is a sales tactic consideration, not a privacy compliance issue.

[OpenAI Usage Policies](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/safety-best-practices)

questions.stem:
A management consultant is preparing a board presentation on three strategic options for market expansion. She uses ChatGPT to generate a slide outline and notices that the structure presents the options in sequential order without a clear framework for comparison.

What should she do to produce a more analytically rigorous outline?

options.text: Ask the model to reduce the number of slides
Ask the model to restructure using a decision matrix framework: define evaluation criteria first, then score each option against those criteria before presenting a recommendation
Ask the model to add visual placeholders for charts
Submit the options in alphabetical order to impose structure

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a decision matrix — criteria defined first, then applied consistently across options — imposes analytical rigor that makes comparisons transparent to the board. Specifying the framework in the prompt gives the model a structure to organize around rather than defaulting to sequential listing.

**A** is incorrect because fewer slides do not make the comparison more rigorous; the analytical framework is what matters.

**C** is incorrect because visual placeholders address presentation design, not analytical structure.

**D** is incorrect because alphabetical ordering imposes an arbitrary sequence that does not provide a comparison framework.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem: A team gets vague responses because prompts omit the user goal, constraints, and desired output shape. What is the MOST direct improvement?

options.text: Provide clear instructions, relevant context, and the expected output format
Reduce every prompt to a few keywords
Assume a follow-up message will fix missing requirements
Increase randomness so the model improvises more

questions.explanation: **Provide clear instructions, relevant context, and the expected output format** is correct because prompt quality improves when the model has explicit goals and constraints. Increasing randomness, underspecifying prompts, or relying on cleanup later does not solve the root problem.

questions.stem: A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. A team gets vague responses because prompts omit the user goal, constraints, and desired output shape. What is the MOST direct improvement?

options.text: Increase randomness so the model improvises more
Reduce every prompt to a few keywords
Provide clear instructions, relevant context, and the expected output format
Assume a follow-up message will fix missing requirements

questions.explanation: **Provide clear instructions, relevant context, and the expected output format** is correct because prompt quality improves when the model has explicit goals and constraints. Increasing randomness, underspecifying prompts, or relying on cleanup later does not solve the root problem.

questions.stem:
A chief of staff needs meeting notes transformed into a concise status update with decisions, risks, and next actions.

Which prompt change BEST improves the result?

options.text: Specify the required sections and audience for the summary
Ask for a summary with no instructions about structure or audience
Increase randomness so the notes become more diverse
Tell the model to respond however it prefers

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because summary quality improves when the desired structure and audience are explicit.

**B** is incorrect because missing structure leaves the model to guess.

**C** is incorrect because higher randomness is not a substitute for clear requirements.

**D** is incorrect because removing guidance reduces consistency.

questions.stem: A journalist is using ChatGPT to help research a historical event. Which TWO practices best protect against publishing inaccurate AI-generated content? (Select TWO)

options.text: Ask the model to list its sources and verify each source independently before citing it
Use only ChatGPT's output because it synthesizes many sources simultaneously
Cross-reference any specific claims, dates, or quotes against primary sources or established news archives
Ask the model to rewrite the content three times to increase accuracy

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because requesting sources from the model and then independently verifying them catches hallucinated citations — a common failure mode where the model generates plausible-but-nonexistent references.

**C** is correct because cross-referencing specific claims (especially dates, quotes, and statistics) against primary sources is the core journalistic standard and the most reliable defense against publishing errors.

**B** is incorrect because synthesis breadth does not guarantee accuracy; the model may synthesize and amplify errors from its training data.

**D** is incorrect because generating more versions of potentially incorrect content does not improve factual accuracy; each version can contain different hallucinations.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A team is reviewing its approach. A support lead wants consistently useful answers from ChatGPT for refund-policy questions. Early outputs are vague because the requests omit the customer type, policy constraints, and expected response format.

Which change MOST directly improves output quality?

options.text: Increase randomness so the model improvises more
Add specific context, constraints, and the desired format to the prompt
Rely on a later follow-up to clarify every missing detail
Replace all prompts with one-word requests

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because clear instructions and relevant context are the foundation of effective prompting.

**A** is incorrect because higher randomness does not compensate for missing instructions.

**C** is incorrect because repeated cleanup is less reliable than providing the needed context upfront.

**D** is incorrect because under-specified prompts usually worsen relevance.

questions.stem:
A product team needs to write a pros and cons analysis of three cloud migration strategies to inform a board decision. An engineering team member drafts an initial list with ChatGPT, but the board chair notes that the analysis seems to favor Strategy B without clear reasoning.

What should the team member do?

options.text: Ask the model to separate factual criteria from assumptions, score each strategy against explicitly defined criteria, and flag where data is incomplete
Switch to Strategy A to remove the appearance of bias
Remove the analysis from the board deck and present verbally instead
Ask the model to confirm that Strategy B is better

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the board chair's concern is about unexplained bias. Adding explicit, weighted criteria and asking the model to distinguish facts from assumptions creates transparency in the evaluation framework and allows the board to judge the analysis independently.

**A** is incorrect because asking the model to confirm a preference compounds the problem; it will generate justification for whatever position it has already taken.

**C** is incorrect because changing the recommendation based on appearance rather than evidence is the opposite of rigorous analysis.

**D** is incorrect because avoiding documentation reduces accountability; a well-structured written analysis is preferable to a verbal presentation for board-level decisions.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
An IT security analyst wants to use ChatGPT to generate sample phishing email templates for use in an authorized internal security awareness training simulation.

What is the MOST appropriate approach?

options.text: Submit the request directly with no additional context, as the intent is benign
Provide clear context in the prompt about the authorized training purpose, and review the output to ensure it is appropriate only for internal simulation and not repurposable for actual attacks
Avoid using AI for this use case entirely because phishing content is never permissible
Ask the model to generate real credential-harvesting links to make the simulation realistic

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because authorized security awareness training is a legitimate use case. Providing explicit context about the purpose helps the model calibrate appropriately, and reviewing output before use ensures the content is suitable for training simulation rather than actual attacks.

**A** is incorrect because the model cannot verify intent, and contextless requests for phishing templates may be declined or produce less useful results.

**C** is incorrect because security awareness training with simulated phishing is a widely accepted practice; the key is authorized, controlled use.

**D** is incorrect because generating functional attack infrastructure (credential-harvesting links) would violate acceptable use policies even in a training context; realistic-looking but inert content is the appropriate approach.

[OpenAI Usage Policies](https://openai.com/policies/usage-policies)

questions.stem:
An account executive pastes a 3,000-word client proposal into ChatGPT and asks: "Is this good?" The model responds with general praise.

What revision would produce the MOST useful evaluation?

options.text: Ask "Is this proposal good or bad?" to force a direct answer
Define specific evaluation criteria such as clarity, value proposition strength, and call-to-action presence, and ask the model to score the proposal on each
Submit the proposal in smaller chunks for individual evaluation
Ask the model to compare the proposal to "industry best practice"

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because defining specific criteria with scorable dimensions gives the model a structured evaluation framework rather than an open-ended quality judgment that defaults to positivity.

**A** is incorrect because a binary good/bad question does not produce actionable feedback for revision.

**C** is incorrect because evaluating chunks in isolation loses the holistic coherence assessment that makes proposal review valuable.

**D** is incorrect because "industry best practice" is vague and subjective; the model's interpretation of that standard may not match the executive's context.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem: An application wants the model to select a business action such as `create_ticket` and return structured arguments for that action. Which feature is designed for this?

options.text: Function calling
A shorter system prompt with no tool definitions
Batch API file upload by itself
Image generation only

questions.explanation: **Function calling** is correct because it lets the model choose from defined tools and emit structured arguments for the selected action. The other options do not define executable actions with arguments.

questions.stem:
A policy writer needs to draft a company AI acceptable use policy. She asks ChatGPT to write the policy from scratch with no additional inputs.

What is the MOST significant limitation of this approach?

options.text: ChatGPT cannot write policy documents

The model will generate a generic policy template that does not reflect the company's specific AI tools, risk profile, industry regulations, or cultural values — significant customization will be required
The policy will be too long for practical use
The model will generate the policy in legal language that employees cannot understand

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a policy generated without company-specific context — which AI tools are deployed, what data is handled, the industry's regulatory environment, and the organization's risk tolerance — will be a generic template requiring substantial rework before adoption.

**A** is incorrect because policy drafting is a common and productive use case for ChatGPT.

**C** is incorrect because length is a stylistic issue that can be addressed with a prompt instruction; it is not the primary limitation.

**D** is incorrect because the model can produce plain-language policies; the issue is content specificity, not language register.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A strategy consultant is using ChatGPT to synthesize insights from five 30-page market research reports into a single 3-page executive brief. After processing, she notices that the brief draws heavily from Report 3 and barely mentions findings from Reports 1 and 5.

What is the MOST likely cause and fix?

options.text: Reduce the target brief length to 1 page
Reports 1 and 5 were not attached correctly; resubmit them

When multiple long documents are in context, the model may weight content from the middle of the context window less heavily; process each report separately and ask the model to synthesize summaries with explicit coverage weighting
Report 3 contains more accurate data than the others; this is expected behavior

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because research shows large language models can underrepresent content from the middle of very long contexts. Processing each report separately into a summary, then synthesizing with explicit instructions to cover all five reports, ensures balanced coverage regardless of document position.

**A** is incorrect because the issue is not about documents missing from the submission; it is about how the model attends to content within a very long context.

**C** is incorrect because the model has no way to evaluate report accuracy independently; it does not preferentially cite more accurate sources.

**D** is incorrect because reducing the target length makes the coverage imbalance worse by providing less space for underrepresented reports.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. A finance team wants AI to approve customer credit decisions without any human review.

Which approach BEST aligns with responsible AI practice?

options.text: Skip review if the model has produced good answers in the past week
Keep a qualified human in the loop for consequential decisions and review outputs before action
Use AI only if the decision can never be audited later
Let the model make final decisions whenever its wording sounds confident

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because high-stakes decisions need human oversight and accountability.

**A** is incorrect because past performance does not remove the need for review in consequential cases.

**C** is incorrect because lack of auditability makes the process riskier, not safer.

**D** is incorrect because confidence in wording is not a control.

questions.stem:
A human resources professional is using ChatGPT to draft interview questions for a senior data analyst role. The first draft contains generic questions suitable for any role.

Which prompt revision would MOST improve relevance?

options.text: Add "make it better" to the original prompt
Specify the seniority level, key responsibilities, required technical skills, and the company's interview philosophy
Ask for exactly 20 questions instead of the current number
Request that the model search the internet for interview templates

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because providing role-specific context — seniority, key responsibilities, required skills, and interview style — gives the model the information it needs to produce targeted, relevant questions.

**A** is incorrect because "make it better" is as vague as the original prompt and gives the model no direction.

**C** is incorrect because changing the quantity does not improve relevance; you will get 20 generic questions instead of fewer generic ones.

**D** is incorrect because standard ChatGPT does not browse the internet (unless browsing is enabled), and the issue is prompt specificity, not content sourcing.

[OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)

questions.stem:
An event planner asks ChatGPT to suggest an agenda for a two-day leadership offsite for 25 senior managers. The model generates a detailed agenda with specific session times, activities, and facilitators.

What critical limitation should the planner keep in mind?

options.text: The agenda may use times that are in a different time zone
The agenda is a generic starting point; session relevance, facilitator availability, budget constraints, and leadership objectives must be validated against the actual event context before use
ChatGPT cannot plan events with more than 10 attendees
The model's agenda will always recommend outdoor activities regardless of weather

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because ChatGPT generates plausible agenda content based on common offsite patterns, not knowledge of the specific leadership team's objectives, existing facilitator relationships, budget, or venue capabilities. Every element requires contextual validation.

**A** is incorrect because the model uses the prompt's implied context for time references; this is a minor formatting issue, not the critical limitation.

**C** is incorrect because there is no attendee limit on ChatGPT's event planning assistance.

**D** is incorrect because the model does not have a preference for outdoor activities; this is a fabricated limitation.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A customer experience team has deployed a custom GPT for handling tier-1 support inquiries. The team wants to ensure that the GPT never discusses competitor products.

Where should this restriction be configured?

options.text: In a follow-up message after each customer inquiry
In the system prompt or custom GPT instructions defined by the operator
In the customer's user profile settings
As a post-processing filter applied to responses before delivery

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the operator — the team deploying the custom GPT — controls the system prompt and custom instructions. This is the appropriate layer for persistent behavioral constraints like topic restrictions that apply to all users of the GPT.

**A** is incorrect because a restriction delivered after each inquiry means the GPT could discuss competitors before the restriction is applied; it also scales poorly.

**C** is incorrect because user profile settings do not typically include GPT behavioral restrictions; and users should not be responsible for enforcing operator-defined boundaries.

**D** is incorrect because post-processing filters can be effective but are a second-line defense; the system prompt is the appropriate primary control point.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)
